title.scholar: Treaty of Versailles and League of Nations Rejected
title.explorer: The Peace Treaty America Said No To!

summary.scholar: The Treaty of Versailles, signed on June 28, 1919, officially ended World War I and imposed harsh penalties on Germany. President Wilson championed the League of Nations as part of the treaty, but the U.S. Senate rejected it in 1919 and 1920, largely due to concerns about entangling alliances. America's refusal to join the League undermined the organization and contributed to the international instability that led to World War II.
summary.explorer: After World War I ended, President Wilson helped write a peace treaty and wanted America to join a new organization called the League of Nations where countries could solve problems peacefully. But the Senate said NO! They didn't want America getting dragged into other countries' problems. Without America, the League was weak, and eventually World War II happened.

significance.scholar: The Senate's rejection of the League of Nations marked a return to isolationism and left the international order without its most powerful potential member.
significance.explorer: America saying no to the League of Nations had huge consequences. Without the strongest country in the world as a member, the League couldn't keep the peace.

sources.citation: Miller Center: U.S. Presidents and Key Events
